it’s probably true a few sanctioned tankers from the old Iran trade found a new home, but claims that we’re seeing a massive, unpriced deluge leaking out onto the water is an absolute stretch.

You’ve got to unpack the structural drivers behind massive MSC JV earlier this year. Sinokor went on an absolute tear sweeping up VLCC capacity—including Dynacom's tonnage—and effectively cornered 20-30% of the entire global spot pool.

These guys are tier one, institutional shipowners operating squarely under the regulatory magnifying glass of Washington and Brussels. The Dark Fleet laundering Iranian barrels runs on rustbuckets—20+ year old disposable hulls wrapped in layers of untraceable shell companies.

If Sinokor and Dynacom started running rogue Iranian crude via STS right in the middle of a US naval blockade with their own corporate flags flying off the stern, secondary sanctions would literally vaporize their entire balance sheets overnight. It’s an untradeable tail risk for them.

What they're running via STS isn't a laundry cycle for dark barrels. It’s almost certainly a logistical re-routing play—shuttling legitimate, stranded barrels out of the chokehold inside the Gulf and over to clean windows in the Gulf of Oman or the Arabian Sea.

Even if you assume the entire inventory delta is dark oil hitting the water, I honestly don't see how a 10mb delta moves the needle in any meaningful way.

Global container freight rates have surged 81% since the outbreak of the Middle East war, with prices on the key China–US routes more than doubling as longer sailing distances, higher fuel costs, and supply chain disruptions continue to strain capacity.

Last week, the Drewry World Container Index jumped 22.6% to a one-year high of USD 3,433 per 40-foot container. The sharpest increases were recorded on transpacific and Asia–North Europe routes, where carriers have successfully pushed through higher rates amid strong demand, war-related surcharges, and ongoing vessel diversions around the Cape of Good Hope.

The latest surge also reflects a rush by shippers to secure capacity ahead of peak-season surcharges due to take effect later this month. With normal shipping patterns yet to be restored and energy costs remaining elevated, freight markets continue to signal that the economic impact of the Middle East disruption extends well beyond the energy sector.

China’s crude oil imports fell to an eight-year low last month at 33.1 million tons (7.8mb/d) as refiners increasingly drew on accumulated inventories rather than sourcing additional barrels from abroad. The 29% YoY drop together with a surge in US exports, releases from SPR, and a degree of demand destruction, helps explain why oil prices failed to rally more aggressively during last month's supply disruptions.
China imported 71.6 million tons in April and May, down  one-quarter compared with the same period last year.

Imports of refined products slumped to a 24-year low of just 1.3m tonnes (approximately 9.6m barrels), a 58% year-on-year decline. During April and May combined, China imported 3.1m tonnes (22.9m barrels), less than half the 6.7m tonnes (49.6m barrels) imported during the same period last year.

China's trade surplus jumped to USD 105.4 billion in May, the biggest since January, as exports jumped more than 19% from a year earlier, topping forecasts as booming demand for artificial intelligence hardware offsets disruptions from the war in Iran, while imports soared over 27%.

Daily Market Report #760

It’s Tuesday. The week started positive, and I am excited to get into the newest updates as always.

It is 2 days until the World Cup, and part of me is ready to let football take over the timeline for a few weeks.

Because the bounce I cautiously wrote about yesterday already has a war pressing on it.

We saw what happened in the Middle East again.

Let’s hope and pray for peace.

Korea had a Black Monday, with the KOSPI halted after falling 10%.

Then the Nasdaq dropped 4%.

Apple also had its yearly conference yesterday, where they showed Siri and AI updates.

And yet, crypto is somehow green this morning.

S&P futures even erased the losses and turned positive.

The disconnect between the chaos and the bounce is really crazy.

Fear & Greed is at 10.

Still pinned in extreme fear.

Bouncing inside maximum fear, to be honest.

Oil rose 5%, back toward the highs and almost touching $100.

Gold is sitting at $4,300.

It is actually down, which is unusual on a war-escalation day and tells you that the de-risking last week was so violent that even gold got sold for liquidity.

Silver is sitting at $73.

The macro this week is all about CPI tomorrow, PPI on Thursday, and jobless claims on Thursday.

After the hot prints that started this whole correction, these numbers decide if the bounce lives or dies.

Bank of America just warned that 70% of its bear-market indicators are now flashing.

Bitcoin is sitting at $63,236, up 0.7% on the day.

The big structural read this morning is brutal and important.

Strategy bought 1,550 BTC, roughly $100M, after selling 32 BTC.

It was a hard move for Michael Saylor to sell, but buying cheaper here answers the capitulation narrative directly.

Glassnode puts the BTC bottom zone between $46K and $54K.

JPMorgan says Strategy needs to increase cash reserves, but the buying directly answers the forced-seller narrative.

They are adding into the worst tape of the cycle.

Ethereum is sitting at $1,686.

ETH dominance is at 9%.

ETH/BTC at 2016 levels is the stat that should worry every ETH holder.

ETH co-founder Joe Lubin had to prop up an ETH margin loan, which is not exactly a good confidence signal.

This is the most hated Ethereum has been in years.

Historically, that kind of sentiment can mark a turn in the market.

But the chart has to prove it first.

Solana $67.01, up 2.4%.

Helius CEO signaled a Solana-exclusive privacy protocol as the next infrastructure priority, which is interesting given everything happening in the privacy sector right now.

The privacy-everywhere thesis keeps spreading to every L1.

XRP $1.17, up 2.1%. BNB $602, up 0.9%. Binance delisting last cycle runners. 

Hyperliquid $61.95, up 2%.

Recovering from the Hayes crash.

Coinbase is now the official deployer of Hyperliquid’s USDC treasury wallet, a real institutional tie-in.

Citrini Research called HYPE a compelling investment, noting that Hyperliquid accounted for nearly half of all crypto token buybacks in 2025.

Hayes still denies buying back in after dumping.

Monero $322, up 5.7%.

Zcash $450, up 5.2%, continuing the recovery from the silent-minting crash.

But here is the unsettling follow-up:

The researcher who found the ZEC Orchard bug is now looking at Monero.

After an Opus 4.8 audit cracked Zcash, the same scrutiny is turning to XMR.

If AI-assisted auditing finds something in Monero, the whole privacy sector gets retested.

Zcash proposed its Ironwood upgrade with formal verification and a supply-audit turnstile, exactly the right response to a counterfeiting scare.

Sui opened confidential transfers for public testing on Devnet.

Umbra launched private bridging across 32 chains.

Privacy is bruised but building faster than ever.

Bittensor $216, up 3.2%. Polkadot $0.97, up 1.2%. Arbitrum $0.0817, flat. Starknet $0.0341, up 1.4%. Pengu $0.0068, up 1.3%, Borg $0.1559, down 1.3%.

The Humanity Protocol hacker minted 100M H tokens worth $11.4M on BSC, and the token collapsed.

That is what happens when an exploit creates unlimited supply.

The NFT whitehat story got even better.

Yuga Labs recovered 65+ NFTs from the Flooring Protocol exploit, including 29 Bored Apes.

Meanwhile, Hyperliquid trader Arthur Huang got liquidated 10 times in 8 hours during the weekend volatility.

SpaceX’s $75B IPO is coming this week and is already 2x oversubscribed.

Kalshi shows Elon Musk’s trillionaire odds at a 97% all-time high.

OpenAI filed a confidential S-1 for its own IPO and is building ChatGPT into a superapp.

Altman is pushing for a US equity-stake backstop, and Trump is meeting AI execs on equity stakes this week.

Sam Altman laid out OpenAI’s 3 goals:

An automated AI researcher.

Accelerating the global economy.

Giving everyone a personal AGI.

The ambition is staggering, and the capital keeps flowing while crypto bleeds.

We built the rails, and AI took the money.

Sam Bankman-Fried formally applied for a presidential pardon from Trump. Can he buy his way out? 

What are you watching this week?

Hours from now, the May inflation number prints, and the whole market will argue about whether the war's oil shock has reached the core. The American consumer already filed an answer, and it is sitting in last week's energy data. Per the EIA, gasolline demand dropped from 9.25 million barrels a day to 8.59 million in a single week, a collapse of roughly 660,000 barrels of daily demand at the start of what should be driving season. Gasoline inventories built from 211.6 to 215 million barrels while crude inventories fell by 8 million. The barrel is tight. The gas tank is full at the depot and half-empty in the car. Per AAA, the pump price has dropped 18 cents in a week to $4.24, the second straight weekly decline, with crude still holding its war premium in the mid-90s. Supply did not improve. Demand quit.

Now, if you read the three panels together, the story has only one author. If the pump price were falling because supply improved, crude inventories would be building. They drained 8 million barrels and sit 3 percent below the five-year average, because the blockade and the strait are still throttling the input. The fuel that exists is piling up at the depot instead, 211.6 to 215 million barrels in a week, because the cars are not showing up to take it. A 660,000 barrel-a-day demand drop at the front edge of the summer driving season is not seasonal noise. It is households looking at $4.24 a gallon, $5.97 in California, and deciding the trip is optional.

The AP's reporting from the retail front lines puts faces on the data. Shoppers are buying gasolline in partial fills, a half tank at a time, managing the expense the way people manage a bill they cannot quite cover. Visits to clothing and furniture stores are thinning. The trade-down to warehouse clubs is accelerating. Per Circana's count, retailers moved 6 percent fewer non-grocery items between April 25 and May 23 than in the same window last year, with housewares, apparel, footwear, and sporting goods down 5 to 7 percent. Walmart, McDonald's, and Dollar General all told analysts the same thing in different words: the aggregate looks resilient, and the bottom income tier is quietly cutting.

I grew up in Pakistan, where fuel queues can decide elections. When the richest consumers on earth start buying gas half a tank at a time, I do not read it as a budgeting tip. I read it as an economy starting to do the Fed's job for it.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗵𝗺𝗲𝘁𝗶𝗰 𝗙𝗼𝗿𝗰𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗜𝘁
None of this behavior needs a survey to explain. Wages are growing 3.4 percent a year. Headline inflation ran 3.95 percent in April and is forecast to print near 4.2 percent tonight. Somewhere in March the two lines crossed, and the average paycheck started shrinking in real terms for the first time since the war began. A household does not need to read the BLS release to feel that. It feels it at the pump, where the most visible price in American life sits a dollar above where the year started, and it responds exactly the way the data shows: fill half the tank, skip the furniture store, drive to the warehouse club.

This is the demand ledger, what the consumer cut: Gasoline demand -660,000 b/d in one week.
Non-grocery units -6% (Apr 25–May 23 vs 2025).
Housewares/clothing/footwear/sports -5% to -7%.
Gas purchase behavior: partial fills. Store mix: trade down to warehouse. 
Who cuts first (per WMT/MCD/DG calls): the lower-income tier. Subtotal: demand destruction, in progress.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗢𝗻𝗹𝘆 𝗖𝗲𝗮𝘀𝗲𝗳𝗶𝗿𝗲 𝗢𝗶𝗹 𝗥𝗲𝘀𝗽𝗲𝗰𝘁𝘀
Here is the part that matters for the screen. Three months of diplomacy, two ceasefires, and a blockade negotiation have failed to durably break the oil premium because the war controls the supply side, and the war is not over. The demand side answers to nobody. When American drivers give up 660,000 barrels a day of consumption, they remove more oil demand in a week than most OPEC quota changes add in a month, and they do it without a single headline. Every oil shock in modern history ended the same way, with the price killing its own customers. The 2008 spike broke at $147 when demand collapsed underneath it. This one is showing the first stress fracture at the pump right now.

For the Federal Reserve, the quit is a trap arriving on schedule. A consumer in retreat cools growth through the exact channel Moody's already has near stall speed at 1.5 percent GDP. The inflation pressing on that consumer comes from a supply shock the Fed cannot drill its way out of. So the economy slows while prices stay hot, the textboook stagflation box, and Warsh walks into his first meeting on June 16-17 with the hawkish case (tonight's CPI) and the recession case (this consumer data) both strengthening at once. A central bank can fight one of those. It cannot fight both with the same tool.

This, for me, is the resilient consumer case, on its merits: The aggregate American consumer is not collapsing. Payrolls grew 172,000 in May, unemployment sits at 4.3 percent, and the same retail executives reporting cutbacks called overall traffic resilient. Weekly EIA demand data is noisy; a single week can mislead, and summer driving could pull some of it back. Higher earners are still spending. The flaw in taking comfort from that resilience is direction: every behavioral indicator in the dataset, fills, units, categories, store mix, is moving the same way at once, and turns that have historically run ahead of the aggregate, not behind it.

The trade implication cuts against my own energy book, and honesty requires saying so. A demand quit puts a ceiling over crude that no Iranian missile can lift, which caps the upside on the oil-floor thesis even while the blockade holds the downside. The cleaner expressions of this dispatch are the ones on either side of the squeezed middle: the discounters and staples that capture the trade-down, and the discretionary names, airlines, and consumer lenders standing where the cuts land. The pump told you before the CPI did. Tonight's print measures the fever. The half tank measures the patient.
